title: The History of the Holy Grail
summary: The checked-in XML is a repository copy of University of Michigan item CME00135. Its FRONT contains only the 1905 EETS Extra Series no. 95 half-title, while its BODY encodes the surviving Middle English verse in Frederick J. Furnivall's four-part EETS edition: first-pagination pp. 100–471 and reset-pagination pp. 1–356. The XML marks the omitted French replacement opening on edition pp. 1–99 with one GAP, and preserves Furnivall's later French supply from Royal MS 14 E III on pp. 304–308, his inserted French explanation of the Turning Isle on pp. 337–340, modern chapter arguments, marginal summaries, variants, and a terminal note from Add MS 10292. Furnivall re-edited the unique Middle English witness, Cambridge, Corpus Christi College, Parker Library, MS 80, whose surviving textual order is now ff. 197r–198v followed by 1r–88v. A complete 414-canvas color Parker facsimile is publicly available and cached at 1600-pixel width. Royal MS 14 E III and Add MS 10292 formerly had complete British Library digital coverage, but their images were unavailable when checked; their catalogs, physical-request form, and paid digitisation route are recorded. Furnivall's 1861–63 Roxburghe Club edition is a parallel earlier edition, not inserted as an undocumented textual intermediary between the manuscript and the EETS re-edition.
review_notes: The intellectual subject is Henry Lovelich's single Middle English verse translation, not the French Estoire, the EETS five-part carrier, Dorothy Kempe's excluded 1905 study, the manuscript codex as a whole, or any digital representation.
The XML has 47 DIV1 elements: one FRONT title-page division plus 46 BODY chapter divisions numbered XI–LVI. These layers are not collapsed into a 47-chapter count.

The BODY begins at source-edition p. 100 with the first surviving Middle English line, 'For that power haven not we'. The GAP EXTENT='99 pages' represents omitted edition pp. 1–99, where Furnivall printed Old French because the English witness begins imperfectly.

A later physical loss is explicit at edition p. 304: Furnivall says the English copyist omitted a column or page. He prints Royal MS 14 E III French through p. 308, and the Middle English resumes on p. 309.

The French prose on edition pp. 337–340 is a different boundary: Furnivall supplies an explanation of the Turning Isle that he says the English translator omitted. It is not silently classified as a lost leaf of CCCC MS 80.
The XML includes 23,784 L elements. DIMEV reports 23,932 manuscript lines and Arlima reports 23,922 verses; the incompatible extents are preserved as an open question rather than normalized.

Parker's MODS record contains both aggregate 'Paper and Vellum' and component-level 'Vellum' material statements. The edition title calls the source a paper manuscript. No unsupported choice among these descriptions is made.
Private Git-ignored caches preserve complete provider objects. The Parker ZIP contains the full 414-canvas codex, not only the target folios; caching does not itself authorize redistribution.
The Parker image sequence follows current foliation, while the work's textual sequence is discontinuous: canvases 401–404 (ff. 197r–198v), then canvases 9–184 (ff. 1r–88v).

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for CME00135
entities.description: The XML artifact consumed by this repository's publication workflow and supplied through the Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule.
notes: IDG/@ID, BIBNO, and VID all identify CME00135; the file is 1,934,015 bytes.

The sole revision entry is dated 2016-08-16 and records MURP structural and proofing work, including typed recovery of six pages missing from the input images; it does not identify the original encoding date or staff.

entities.label: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English item CME00135
entities.description: An electronic transcription of selected Furnivall/Kempe EETS carrier matter: one 1905 series half-title and the surviving Middle English verse edition on first-pagination pp. 100–471 and reset-pagination pp. 1–356, with inherited editorial French, English paratext, apparatus, page milestones, line milestones, and italic signals.
notes: TEXT/@LANG is 'enm fre'. Old French occurs in Furnivall's editorial supply and comparison layers; modern English occurs in the retained half-title, chapter arguments, summaries, and notes.
The stable name route identifies the item; direct automated inspection of the item was unavailable, while the collection page and repository XML were inspectable.

entities.label: The History of the Holy Grail
entities.description: Henry Lovelich's Middle English verse translation and adaptation of the Old French Vulgate Estoire del Saint Graal, narrating the origins and transmission of the Grail through Joseph of Arimathea, Josephes, converted Saracen rulers, and their descendants, and foreshadowing the later Arthurian Grail quest. The surviving text begins imperfectly.

date_statements.label: Composition and translation
date_statements.notes: The Database of Middle English Romance localizes composition to London; this is distinct from the later production date and Essex dialect localization of the surviving manuscript.

notes: No manuscript title is asserted. The preferred title is a modern scholarly/cataloging title.
Lovelich's closing lines name him and say that he drew the story into English; nineteenth-century title-page forms 'Lonelich' are edition-layer spellings.

entities.label: Estoire del Saint Graal
entities.description: The Old French prose Vulgate-cycle Grail history from which Lovelich translated and versified his Middle English work. The exact physical French exemplar used by Lovelich is unidentified.
notes: The medieval English colophon and nineteenth-century editions associate Robert de Boron with translation from Latin into French. Modern source identification here remains at the anonymous Vulgate Estoire work level and does not convert that claim into confirmed modern authorship.

entities.label: F. J. Furnivall, The History of the Holy Grail, Parts I–IV, EETS Extra Series 20, 24, 28, and 30 (1874–1878)
entities.description: Furnivall's four-part EETS textual edition re-edits Lovelich's Middle English verse from CCCC MS 80, supplies the lost opening and a later lacuna from Royal MS 14 E III, and collates French manuscripts. CME00135's BODY derives from selected pages of these four parts; Dorothy Kempe's separately issued 1905 Part V study and retained half-title are modeled as a different publication entity.

bibliographic.citation: The History of the Holy Grail, Englisht, ab. 1450 A.D., by Herry Lonelich, skynner, from the French prose of Sires Robiers de Borron, re-edited from the unique manuscript by Frederick J. Furnivall, Parts I–IV, EETS Extra Series 20, 24, 28, 30 (London: N. Trübner & Co., 1874, 1875, 1877, 1878).
bibliographic.title: The History of the Holy Grail
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Extra Series 20, 24, 28, 30
bibliographic.pages: Part I pp. 1–172; Part II pp. 173–471; Part III pp. 1–208; Part IV pp. 209–361

notes: The Middle English poem ends on Part IV p. 356; the Merlin excerpt on pp. 357–361 is not encoded.
CME00135's BODY selects Part I pp. 100–172, all Part II pp. 173–471, all Part III pp. 1–208, and Part IV pp. 209–356.

entities.label: Dorothy Kempe, The Legend of the Holy Grail: Its Sources, Character and Development, EETS Extra Series 95 (1905)
entities.description: Dorothy Kempe's separately issued General Introduction and Part V study. CME00135 retains only its EETS series half-title and excludes the title page, introduction, study, and other substantive matter; it is therefore not treated as co-edited continuation of Furnivall's textual Parts I–IV.

bibliographic.citation: Dorothy Kempe, The Legend of the Holy Grail: Its Sources, Character and Development, The Introduction to, and Part V of, Henry Lovelich's Verse History of the Holy Grail, EETS Extra Series 95 (London: Kegan Paul, Trench, Trübner & Co., 1905).
bibliographic.title: The Legend of the Holy Grail: Its Sources, Character and Development
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Extra Series 95

notes: Only the half-title reading 'The Legend of the Holy Grail / EETS / Extra Series No XCV 1905' is retained in CME00135 FRONT.

entities.label: Google / Internet Archive bound digital object historyholygrai01lovegoog
entities.description: A complete 570-page provider reproduction containing Furnivall's EETS Parts I–II with intervening and surrounding carrier matter. CME00135's BODY occupies Part I pp. 100–172 and Part II pp. 173–471 within this object.
entities.reproduction_format: Google-derived page images distributed by Internet Archive as PDF, OCR text/XML, scandata, and metadata

entities.label: Google / Internet Archive bound digital object historyholygrai00lovegoog
entities.description: A complete 436-page provider reproduction binding Furnivall's EETS Parts III–IV, reports and advertisements, and Kempe's separately issued Part V study. CME00135 derives its remaining BODY from Parts III–IV and its FRONT half-title alone from the Part V portion.
entities.reproduction_format: Google-derived page images distributed by Internet Archive as PDF, OCR text/XML, scandata, and metadata

entities.label: Cambridge, Corpus Christi College, Parker Library, MS 80
entities.description: The unique manuscript witness of Lovelich's History of the Holy Grail and Merlin. The Grail text begins imperfectly on two displaced leaves now ff. 197–198, originally leaves 10–11 of quire B, and then continues on ff. 1r–88v. It is written in double columns in a current hand.

date_statements.label: Manuscript production
date_statements.notes: Parker's summary uses c. 1450–75, while the encoded M. R. James date field and origin range are broadly fifteenth century.

holding.notes: The History of the Holy Grail component is cataloged at ff. 1r–88v with the displaced surviving opening at ff. 197r–198v; Merlin continues from f. 88v to 200v.

physical_description.support: Parker MODS gives both 'Paper and Vellum' and 'Vellum'; the EETS title describes a paper manuscript
physical_description.format: Codex; double columns of 65, 69, 74, and varying numbers of lines; current hand
notes: Collation: quire A gone; quire B of 16 wants leaves 1–9, while leaves 10–11 are now ff. 197–198 and leaves 12–16 remain in situ.
Vellum guards preserve a twelfth-century manuscript with lessons from I Kings; later marginal notes include writing by John Cok.
Parker considers it likely that the manuscript remained in or around London before entering Matthew Parker's collection.
DIMEV associates the manuscript language with LALME/eLALME linguistic profile 6360, grid 557 181, Essex. That is a dialect localization of the surviving copy, not the London composition claim.
The surviving work locus in textual order is ff. 197r–198v, then 1r–88v; the current codex order is not the narrative order.

entities.label: Parker Library complete digital facsimile of CCCC MS 80
entities.description: A complete 414-canvas IIIF Presentation 2 color sequence from the outside front binding through the outside back binding. The private cache bundles the provider manifest, an inventory, and all 414 canvases requested at 1600-pixel width.
entities.reproduction_format: IIIF Presentation 2 manifest and JPEG image services; cached as a 416-member ZIP containing manifest, inventory, and 414 images
notes: This is complete to the 414-canvas published manifest, not a preservation master or the higher-resolution commercial-publication service.
The target textual order maps to canvases 401–404 and then 9–184.

entities.label: London, British Library, Royal MS 14 E III
entities.description: An illuminated Old French Lancelot-Grail manuscript whose incomplete Estoire del Saint Graal occupies ff. 3r–88v. Furnivall used it to supply the lost opening, a later English lacuna, and explanatory/comparative French in the EETS edition.

holding.notes: The Estoire del Saint Graal is incomplete on ff. 3r–88v; the codex also contains La Queste del Saint Graal and an abridged Morte Artu.

physical_description.format: Codex; three columns of 51 lines; Gothic script; 116 miniatures

notes: The BL catalog records full historical digital coverage but currently labels its images unavailable. A letter of introduction is required for physical consultation.

entities.label: London, British Library, Add MS 10292
entities.description: An illuminated Old French Lancelot-Grail volume dated 1316. Its Estoire del Saint Graal occupies ff. 1r–76r. Furnivall cites it extensively for variants, chapter headings, illustration rubrics, and the terminal French explicit.

holding.notes: L'Estoire del Saint Graal is ff. 1r–76r; L'Estoire de Merlin follows on ff. 76r–216v.

physical_description.format: Codex; three columns of 50 lines; Gothic script; 233 miniatures

notes: The BL catalog records historical digitisation but currently labels images unavailable. A letter of introduction is required for physical consultation.

entities.label: F. J. Furnivall, Seynt Graal, or The Sank Ryal (Roxburghe Club, 1861–1863)
entities.description: Furnivall's earlier two-volume edition prints Lovelich's Middle English verse together with Old French prose and substantial prefatory and appendix matter. The later EETS publication is explicitly a re-edition without the continuous French parallel; the EETS text nevertheless states that it was re-edited from the unique manuscript, so this earlier edition is modeled as a parallel edition rather than a proven intervening transcription.

bibliographic.citation: Seynt Graal, or The Sank Ryal: The History of the Holy Graal, Partly in English Verse, by Henry Lonelich, and Wholly in French Prose, edited by Frederick J. Furnivall, 2 vols (London: J. B. Nichols and Sons for the Roxburghe Club, 1861–1863).
bibliographic.title: Seynt Graal, or The Sank Ryal

entities.label: Cornell / Internet Archive digital object cu31924013114602
entities.description: A complete 614-page provider reproduction of volume 1 of Furnivall's 1861–1863 Roxburghe Club edition, including its Middle English and parallel Old French text and surrounding carrier matter.
entities.reproduction_format: Page-image PDF, OCR XML, and provider metadata

entities.label: Cornell / Internet Archive digital object cu31924064990850
entities.description: A complete 472-page provider reproduction of volume 2 of Furnivall's 1861–1863 Roxburghe Club edition. The Grail edition concludes on printed p. 391 and is followed by appendix matter.
entities.reproduction_format: Page-image PDF, OCR XML, and provider metadata

entities.label: DIMEV record 1386 / IMEV and NIMEV 842.5
entities.description: Versioned scholarly index data for the poem, its incipit, author, title, Arthurian subject, two-line aa form, unique witness, displaced leaves, manuscript extent, dialect profile, and Furnivall editions.

rights.component: Modern XML markup and repository-specific contribution
rights.basis: The pinned CME repository contains an Unlicense text dedicating 'software' to the public domain, but it does not expressly define whether imported textual encodings and third-party contributions are included. Medieval and nineteenth-century textual layers are separately scoped.
notes: The scope ambiguity is reported rather than resolved by inference.

rights.component: Medieval Old French Estoire del Saint Graal literary content
rights.basis: The Old French work and the fourteenth-century Royal and Additional witnesses recorded here are centuries outside copyright term. Modern editions, photographs, and provider terms remain separately scoped.

editorial_practices.source_feature: A unique English witness beginning imperfectly, with a later copyist omission and another source episode omitted in translation
editorial_practices.representation: Surviving English verse supplemented or explained by Old French prose from Royal MS 14 E III
editorial_practices.description: Furnivall prints Old French on pp. 1–99 for the lost opening, on pp. 304–308 for a later English lacuna, and on pp. 337–340 for the Turning Isle exposition omitted by the English translator. These three boundaries have different textual causes.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Middle English CCCC MS 80 and Old French Royal 14 E III / Add 10292 readings
editorial_practices.representation: Footnotes, marginal variants, chapter-rubric comparisons, French source blocks, and terminal Add 10292 explicit
editorial_practices.description: The EETS pages preserve a dense source apparatus rather than a manuscript-only diplomatic transcript. Add 10292 and Royal readings must not be mistaken for text present in CCCC MS 80.
editorial_practices.scope: Parts I–IV and CME00135 NOTE/P/TAILNOTE layers

editorial_practices.source_feature: Furnivall's four textual-edition parts plus Kempe's separately issued Part V study, now represented in two provider-bound objects with intervening reports and advertisements
editorial_practices.representation: One Kempe Part V half-title plus the surviving verse run from Furnivall Parts I–IV on pp. 100–471 and reset pp. 1–356
editorial_practices.description: The encoding excludes the French opening on Furnivall Part I pp. 1–99 except for a GAP, Part IV pp. 357–361, and Kempe's substantive Part V study, while retaining Kempe's series half-title and Furnivall's later French/editorial blocks embedded within the selected verse run.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Italic letters and spans in Furnivall's print, including many expansion-like signals and source-language typography
editorial_practices.description: The XML explicitly retains Furnivall's italic typography. Individual spans can be compared with the Parker facsimile, but no global editorial statement was found proving that every italic span maps one-to-one to a manuscript abbreviation; the markup is therefore not decoded more narrowly.
editorial_practices.scope: Italic HI spans throughout Middle English, French, notes, and paratext
notes: The complete facsimile permits folio-level study of particular expansions, including the displaced opening leaves.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Printed main verse, chapter arguments, marginal summaries, footnotes, page numbers, manuscript folio/column markers, and editorial French blocks
editorial_practices.representation: LG/L, ARGUMENT/P, NOTE, PB, MILESTONE, direct P, and TAILNOTE elements
editorial_practices.description: The encoding serializes several nineteenth-century editorial and physical-reference layers alongside the medieval verse. NOTE and MILESTONE counts therefore document apparatus density, not additional literary lines or witnesses.

evidence.citation: General Corpus, CME/source/CME_phase_3/CME00135.xml at CME commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.locator: IDG; FRONT; BODY chapters XI–LVI; GAP; PB, MILESTONE, NOTE, P, LG/L, and TAILNOTE elements
evidence.summary: Primary local evidence for item identity, retained half-title, precise source selection, chapter hierarchy, Middle English verse, French supplied and explanatory blocks, editorial arguments and notes, incipit, explicit, page mappings, manuscript foliation milestones, and encoded italic signals.

evidence.citation: Structural and text-node audit of CME00135.xml.
evidence.locator: Element counts, division hierarchy, PB sequences, direct BODY/P blocks, and boundary text
evidence.summary: Confirms 47 DIV1 total: one FRONT title-page division and 46 BODY chapters XI–LVI; 45 HEAD, 60 P, 49 LG, 23,784 L, 730 PB total / 729 BODY PB, 4,708 NOTE, 6,088 MILESTONE, and one 99-page GAP. Four BODY chapters contain direct editorial prose blocks: French in XXIV, XXV, and XXVII, and a bracketed English summary in XXXVI.

evidence.citation: Kyvero-Press/CME, LICENSE at commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.locator: Unlicense text and its reference to software
evidence.summary: Supports the repository's Unlicense statement while leaving open whether imported text encodings fall within its word 'software'.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse collection page.
evidence.locator: Collection description and Rights and Permissions
evidence.summary: Current official evidence that the collection was manually keyed from public-domain editions, uses basic TEI semantics, offers bulk XML, is publicly accessible, and applies No Copyright–United States.

evidence.citation: Frederick J. Furnivall, ed., The History of the Holy Grail, Parts I–IV, EETS Extra Series 20, 24, 28, 30 (1874–1878).
evidence.locator: Part title pages; Part I pp. 1–172; Part II pp. 173–471; Part III pp. 1–208; Part IV pp. 209–361
evidence.summary: Primary textual-edition evidence for Furnivall's responsibility, EETS series numbers, publication dates, pagination, direct re-edition from the unique manuscript, French opening and later supplies, variant witnesses, chapter paratext, work conclusion on p. 356, and excluded Merlin appendix.

evidence.citation: Dorothy Kempe, The Legend of the Holy Grail: Its Sources, Character and Development, EETS Extra Series 95 (1905).
evidence.locator: Part V series half-title at PDF p. 390 / Internet Archive page n389; title page and General Introduction following
evidence.summary: Primary evidence that Kempe's 1905 Part V is a separately titled study and that CME00135 retains only its series half-title while excluding its substantive title page, General Introduction, and study.

evidence.citation: Page and OCR inspection of the two complete Google/Internet Archive bound scans containing EETS Parts I–V.
evidence.locator: First scan PDF 163–559; second scan PDF 12–219, 236–383, and 390
evidence.summary: Confirms the two distinct provider objects and their exact coverage: Parts I–II in historyholygrai01lovegoog; Furnivall Parts III–IV, intervening carrier matter, and Kempe Part V in historyholygrai00lovegoog. It also confirms exact CME BODY and FRONT boundaries.

evidence.citation: Internet Archive metadata for historyholygrai01lovegoog and historyholygrai00lovegoog.
evidence.locator: metadata and files inventories
evidence.summary: Supports identifiers, 570- and 436-image complete scan objects, Google scanning and source book IDs, exact derivative filenames, public downloads, bibliographic description, and NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT / copyright-region US provider assertions.

evidence.citation: Parker Library on the Web / Stanford Libraries, MODS record for Cambridge, Corpus Christi College, MS 080.
evidence.locator: Title, summary, physical description, collation, component records, access conditions, and license
evidence.summary: Official evidence for shelfmark, unique Lovelich Grail and Merlin texts, c. 1450–75 summary, broad fifteenth-century date, dimensions, double-column layout, material-description conflict, quire loss and displaced ff. 197–198, target locus, London/Skinners context, and CC BY-NC 4.0 image terms with commercial-publication contact.

evidence.citation: M. R. James catalogue record for Cambridge, Corpus Christi College, MS 80, distributed by Parker Library on the Web.
evidence.locator: MS 80 description, collation, contents, and historical bibliography
evidence.summary: Supporting catalog evidence for the physical codex, foliation, displaced leaves, work boundaries, and Furnivall editions.

evidence.citation: Parker Library on the Web, IIIF Presentation 2 manifest for CCCC MS 80.
evidence.locator: 414 canvases, labels, image services, attribution, and license
evidence.summary: Confirms a complete 414-canvas sequence, exact canvas/image-service mappings, public image delivery, and CC BY-NC 4.0 attribution and higher-resolution publication contact.

evidence.citation: Sequence and representative-image inspection of the complete Parker CCCC MS 80 IIIF bundle.
evidence.locator: Canvases 401–404 and 9–184
evidence.summary: Confirms exact work endpoints and the discontinuous textual sequence: f. 197r/canvas 401 through f. 198v/canvas 404, then f. 1r/canvas 9 through f. 88v/canvas 184. The complete bundle contains all 414 manifest canvases and passes ZIP integrity testing.

evidence.citation: Database of Middle English Romance, 'History of the Holy Grail'.
evidence.locator: Date, place, form, IMEV, keywords, manuscript table, source statement, summary, and edition list
evidence.summary: Scholarly evidence for 1420s London composition, rhyming-couplet form, IMEV 842.5, sole CCCC MS 80 witness at ff. 197r–198v and 1r–88v, translation from the Vulgate Estoire, Furnivall's Royal MS 14 E III supplies, narrative subjects, and four EETS edition parts.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse, version 1.0, record 1386 / IMEV and NIMEV 842.5, with linked manuscript and bibliography data.
evidence.locator: Records.xml record-1386; Manuscripts.xml and MSSIndex.xml CamCCC80; Bibliography.xml Furnivall1861 and Furnivall1874-78
evidence.summary: Versioned evidence for incipit, Lovelich authorship, title, Arthurian subject, two-line aa couplets, unique witness, displaced ff. 197–198 then ff. 1–88v, imperfect beginning, reported 23,932-line extent, EETS and Roxburghe editions, and LALME/eLALME LP 6360 Essex dialect localization. The release includes CC BY 4.0 license text.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Royal MS 14 E III.
evidence.locator: Scope and content; ff. 3r–88v Estoire; date; origin; physical characteristics; access; digitised-content status
evidence.summary: Official evidence for the incomplete Estoire component, c. 1315–1325 production, North France origin, parchment codex, dimensions and layout, current physical-request conditions, letter-of-introduction requirement, and formerly complete but currently unavailable digital images.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Add MS 10292.
evidence.locator: Scope and content; ff. 1r–76r Estoire; 1316 date; physical characteristics; access; digitised-content status
evidence.summary: Official evidence for the Estoire component, exact 1316 date, North France origin, parchment codex, dimensions and layout, current physical-request conditions, letter-of-introduction requirement, and formerly complete but currently unavailable digital images.

evidence.citation: British Library, Digitisation services and 2025/26 digitisation and reproduction rate card.
evidence.locator: Online ordering; required information; manuscript photography; color and surrogate notes; licensing; rate card pp. 1–2
evidence.summary: Current official evidence for the online paid digitisation route, required shelfmark/title/folio-side details, reference versus reproduction formats, manuscript photography rather than scanning, possible microfilm substitution, need to request color, publication-permission warning, licensing email, and current per-image rates.

evidence.citation: Frederick J. Furnivall, ed., Seynt Graal, or The Sank Ryal, 2 vols (London: J. B. Nichols and Sons for the Roxburghe Club, 1861–1863).
evidence.locator: Volume title pages, prefaces, Middle English and Old French parallel text, appendices
evidence.summary: Primary evidence for the first verified publication of Lovelich's work in a two-volume edition, Furnivall responsibility, Roxburghe Club publication, 1861/1863 dates, English/French parallel presentation, and accompanying essays and appendices.

evidence.citation: Cornell University Library / Internet Archive complete scans of Seynt Graal volumes 1 and 2.
evidence.locator: Complete 614-page and 472-page PDF objects and OCR
evidence.summary: Supports two distinct complete provider reproductions of the Roxburghe edition volumes and permits comparison against Furnivall's later EETS re-edition. Both cached PDFs pass qpdf structural checks.

evidence.citation: Internet Archive metadata for cu31924013114602 and cu31924064990850.
evidence.locator: metadata and file inventories
evidence.summary: Supports Cornell provenance, public files, identifiers, complete source-image counts, bibliographic notes, and the no-known-US-copyright statement visible in the two digital objects.

open_questions.question: Why do the checked XML's 23,784 L elements, DIMEV's 23,932 manuscript lines, and Arlima's 23,922 verses disagree?
next_steps: Collate the XML L sequence against Furnivall Parts I–IV and the Parker images, preserving the pp. 304–308 French lacuna and pp. 337–340 translator-omission insertion as non-English layers.
Determine the counting conventions behind DIMEV and Arlima before treating either number as a manuscript line count.
Do not infer missing XML verse solely from aggregate differences.

open_questions.question: Which physical Old French Estoire manuscript or textual branch did Lovelich use, and how should the medieval Robert de Boron attribution be evaluated?
next_steps: Consult modern textual studies of Lovelich and the Vulgate Estoire rather than treating Royal 14 E III or Add 10292 as his exemplar merely because Furnivall used them.
Compare Lovelich's readings and omissions against multiple French Estoire witnesses.
Keep the colophon's Robert de Boron claim as medieval attribution history until modern source criticism supports a narrower responsibility statement.

open_questions.question: Will the British Library restore public complete facsimiles for Royal MS 14 E III and Add MS 10292, and do stable IIIF or downloadable replacements exist?
next_steps: Recheck both current BL catalog records and legacy Digitised Manuscripts routes after service-recovery updates.
Ask BL Reference Services whether historical digital files or new IIIF manifests can be supplied.
Use the paid digitisation route for exact folios only if public restoration remains unavailable and costs/permissions are acceptable.
notes: Both current catalog records explicitly say digital images are unavailable; historical full-coverage notes are not current download links.

open_questions.question: Is CCCC MS 80's principal support paper, vellum, or a mixed material description at different cataloging levels?
next_steps: Ask Parker Library staff to reconcile the MODS aggregate 'Paper and Vellum' statement, its component 'Vellum' statement, and Furnivall's 'paper MS' title.
Consult current conservation/codicological documentation rather than choosing a term from visual appearance alone.
Preserve the conflicting source statements in the meantime.

open_questions.question: When, by whom, and from which physical or digital copy of the EETS edition was CME00135 originally keyed?
next_steps: Ask U-M Digital Collections for item-level production records and source-copy identifiers.
Compare distinctive scan defects and corrected readings against available EETS copies.
Keep the current artifact-to-U-M link supported rather than claiming an undocumented scanner or exact copy.
